Com m issioIL 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
William M. Smiland 
11319 Homedale Street 
Los Angeles, CA 
90049 
Dear Mr. Smiland: 
Executive/legol 
•• 
Enforcement 
321-5901 
322-6441 
November 2, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
South Coast Air Quality 
Management District Board 
Advice Letter No. A-83-225 
This is in response to your written comments to my 
October 12, 1983, letter. 
In your letter you made the following 
statements: 
1. 
As of December 31, 1982, the book value of your capital 
account in your law firm was more than 10 percent of 
the book value of the firm's total capital. 
In 
addition, your interest in the firm's profits and 
losses is more than 10 percent (although not 
necessarily the same percentage as your capital 
interest) . 
2. 
Your firm last received a payment from the Ad Hoc 
Committee of Small California Paint Manufacturers more 
than 12 months prior to your appointment. 
Since that 
last payment, certain fees were billed, but remain 
unpaid, and other fees were accrued, but remain 
unbilled. 
Comment: 
In my letter, I assumed that the Ad Hoc Committee 
of Small California Manufacturers was a source of 
income to you of $250 or more during the last 12 
months. 
The above facts do not alter this assumption. 
The phrase "source of income" includes any person or 
entity which provides or promises income to the 
official aggregating $250 or. more within the 12 months 
pr ior to a dec is ion. 
(Governrnen t Code Sect ion 


William M. Smiland 
November 2, 1983 
Page 2 
87103{c) .)1/ The fees accrued by the Ad Hoc 
Committee constitute a "promise" to pay your law firm 
for services the firm provided. 
3. 
Your firm resigned its representation of the Ad Hoc 
Committee as of June 1, 1983, one day after the 
confirmation of your appointment. 
Your family's paint 
companies are members of the committee and it is your 
feeling that the interests of those businesses and the 
interests of the committee and its other members are 
substantially identical insofar as the District's rules 
are concerned. 
Comment: 
In my letter, I discussed the disqualification 
provisions of the Act. 
In determining whether you must 
abstain from a particular decision, you must determine 
the decision's foreseeable effect both on the paint 
companies and on the Ad Hoc Committee. 
Because the 
interests of each a~e substantially identical, many 
decisions which require your disqualification may 
materially affect both interests. 
4. 
You questioned my statement that you and your firm 
"lobbied" on behalf of the Ad Hoc Committee. 
In using 
this term, I was not stat~ng that you or the firm 
qualified as lobbyists within the meaning of the Act. 
(Section 82039; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239.) 
I 
used this term in the sense that you and your firm 
"advocated" the Ad Hoc Committee's position. 
I will attach your letter and a copy of this letter to my 
correspondence of October 12, 1983. 
If you have any additional 
comments or questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division

Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


tate of Califorrtia 
air Political Practices Commissiort 
BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration

Executive/legal

Enforcement 
(916) 322·5662 
322·5660 
32?·5901 
322·6441 
October 12, 1983 
Statements af Economic Interest 
322·6444 
William M. Smiland 
11319 Homedale Street 
Los Angeles, CA 
90049 
Re: 
South Coast Air Quality 
Management District Board 
Advice Letter No. A-83-225 
Dear Mr. Smiland: 
Ms. Milman and I were pleased to have an opportunity to meet 
with you and Mr. Coleman on September 28, 1983. 
This letter is 
to confirm our advice to you. 
As we discussed at our meeting, the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act prohibit you from making or participating 
in the making of a governmental decision in which you know or 
have reason to know that you have a financial interest. 
You 
have a "financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of 
Government Code Section 8710011 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, 
on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which you have a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any source of income, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to you 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made. 
(See Section 87103.) 
During our meeting, you stated that you have an investment 
interest in a paint manufacturing company, a paint retailing 
company, and a law firm. 
For purposes of our discussion, you 
asked that we assume that your investment interest in the law 
firm amounted to 10 percent or more. 
The firm was a source of 
11 
Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


William M. Smiland 
October 12, 1983 
Page 2 
income to you of $250 or more in the last 12 months. 
In 
addition, there were some clients who, within the last 12 
months, paid the firm a sufficient amount of fees, so that your 
pro rata share of their fees was $250 or more. 
(See Government 
Code Section 82030.) 
One such client was the Ad Hoc Committee 
of Small California Paint Manufacturers, for whom you lobbied on 
matters concerning the regulation of the paint industry. 
The questions you presented, and our responses are as 
follows: 
1. 
Must you disqualify yourself from a decision which 
affects products and services which are not furnished by the 
paint companies in which you have an interest, but which are 
furnished by competing companies? 
The Political Reform Act only requires you to disqualify 
yourself from a decision affecting a competitor if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
effect on any of the paint companies in which you have a 
financial interest. 
(See Sections 87100 and 87103.) 
In most 
instances, decisions affecting your competitor will have little 
or no affect on your companies. 
This is especially true where 
the product or service affected is not offered by one of your 
companies. 
However, it is possible that a decision affecting a 
competitor might materially affect your interests. 
For example, 
a decision which limited your competitor's ability to sell a 
particular product might, in some situations, foreseeably cause 
enough consumers to switch to a subsitute product offered by one 
of your companies to have a material effect on your company. 
2. 
Must you disqualify yourself from a decision which 
affects a product or service which your companies do not 
furnish, but which are possible substitutes for products or 
services which your companies do furnish? 
As discussed in Question #2, you are only required to 
disqualify yourself from a decision affecting a substitute 
product or service if the decision will foreseeably have a 
material financial effect on one of the paint companies in which 
you have an interest. 
In most decisions, this will not be the 
case. 
3. 
As a Board member with a financial interest in a company 
subject to District regulation, must you disqualify yourself 
from decisions concerning fees and budgetary allocations? 
You stated that most of the District's money is raised by 
permit and emission fees. 
Under the "public generally 


William M. Smiland 
October 12, 1983 
Page 3 
exception" (see Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703), you are only required to disqualify yourself from a 
decision concerning the fees if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
of your businesses that is different from the effect on all or 
most other businesses regulated by the Board. 
(For example, you 
would have to disqualify yourself from a decision to increase 
the permit fee for paint manufacturers, but to leave the fees 
for other kinds of businesses intact.) 
However, if the 
decision's effect on your companies is substantially similar to 
the effect on all or most other regulated businesses, you are 
permitted to make or participate in the decision. 
(For example, 
you would not have to disqualify yourself from a decision to 
impose a $5,000 annual fee on all of the businesses regulated by 
the Board.) 
With respect to the District's budget, it seems unlikely 
that a decision regarding an allocation of money to any of the 
budget's general categories (such as public relations, 
enforcement, or air quality monitoring) will foreseeably have a 
material financial effect on your interests which is 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. 
However, is such an effect is foreseeable, you would have to 
disqualify yourself. 
You should consult the advice letter to 
Kenneth Moy which discusses how disqualification may be handled 
in such a situation. 
(Ms. Milman provided a copy of this letter 
to your counsel, Mr. Coleman.) 
4. 
As a Board member with an interest in a "small business" 
(within the meaning of Health and Safety Code Sections 40403 and 
40448), must you disqualify yourself from Board decisions 
affecting "small businesses"? 
You are not required to disqualify yourself from policy 
decisions concerning the handling of small businesses regulated 
by the Board because such decisions affect a significant segment 
of the public generally in substantially the same manner. 
However, you might be required to disqualify yourself from a 
decision affecting only small paint manufacturing or retail 
businesses. 
The "public generally" exception does not apply 
because a particular industry does not constitute a significant 
segment of the public generally. 
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703.) 
5. 
Must you disqualify yourself from decisions concerning 
either rulemaking philosophy or methodology (e.g. decisions 
regarding the propriety of relying on variances, or the nature 
or quantity of proof needed to justify a regulation), or 
questions of law governing rulemaking? 


William M. Smiland 
October 12, 1983 
Page 4 
A decision regaralng either rulemaking philosophy or 
methodology, or questions of law will not require your 
disqualification unless it arises directly in connection with a 
decision in which you have a financial interest. 
6. 
Must you report any changes in your financial interests 
between the filings of your Statements of Economic Interests? 
The Political Reform Act does not require filers to disclose 
changes in financial interests between filings. 
Any changes 
will be reported on your next annual Statement of Economic 
Interest s. 
7. 
If you determine that you should disqualify yourself 
from a decision, what is the proper language for announcing a 
disqualification? 
Once you determine that disqualification is required, this 
determination, plus a disclosure of your financial interest, 
must be made part of the agency's official record. 
(2 Cal. Adm. 
Cod e Sec t ion 18 70 0 ( b) (5) .) 
During our conversation, we also discussed the fact that the 
Ad Hoc Committee of Small California Paint Manufacturers was a 
source of income to you of $250 or more during the last 12 
months. 
Thus, you must disqualify yourself from any decision 
which will foreseeably have a material financial affect on this 
group. 
You are also required to disqualify yourself from a 
decision if there is a nexus between the governmental decision 
and the purpose for which you received the income. 
In other 
words, you may not accomplish in your public capacity what you 
were paid to do in your private capacity. 
For example if, in 
the last 12 months, you were paid to lobby against a specific 
regulation, you cannot now vote on that regulation as a member 
of the Board. 
(See the enclosed copy of the letter to Linda 
Best. ) 
If, in the future, we can be of any additional help to you, 
please feel free to contact our office at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
~

Janis Shank McLean

During our conversation, we also discussed the fact that the

regulation, you cannot now vote on that regulation as a member 
of the Board. 
(See the enclosed copy of the letter to Linda 
Best. ) 
If, in the future, we can be of any additional help to you,
sections.conclusion: Disqualification is required only when decisions will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the official's financial interests that is distinguishable from effects on the public generally. The "public generally" exception applies to decisions affecting all or most regulated businesses similarly. Disqualification must include disclosure of the financial interest and be made part of the agency's official record. Regarding the Ad Hoc Committee of Small California Paint Manufacturers as a source of income, disqualification is required for decisions with material financial effects on that group or where there is a nexus between the decision and the purpose for which income was received.
sections.question_synthetic: William Smiland, a board member of the South Coast Air Quality Management District with financial interests in paint companies and a law firm, seeks guidance on when he must disqualify himself from board decisions involving competitors, substitute products, fees, budgetary matters, small business regulations, rulemaking methodology, and the proper procedure for announcing disqualifications.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advises that disqualification is required only when a decision will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the official's interests that differs from its effect on the public generally or most other regulated businesses. The Ad Hoc Committee constitutes a source of income requiring disqualification for decisions materially affecting that group, and there is a nexus prohibition preventing accomplishment of privately-paid advocacy through public office.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document consists of two letters: October 12, 1983 initial advice letter and November 2, 1983 follow-up response addressing Smiland's comments. Both letters included in document. Some OCR artifacts present but content is clear. The Question and Conclusion sections are clearly defined and verbatim extracted.

CONCLUSION: Disqualification is required only when decisions will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the official's financial interests that is distinguishable from effects on the public generally. The "public generally" exception applies to decisions affecting all or most regulated businesses similarly. Disqualification must include disclosure of the financial interest and be made part of the agency's official record. Regarding the Ad Hoc Committee of Small California Paint Manufacturers as a source of income, disqualification is required for decisions with material financial effects on that group or where there is a nexus between the decision and the purpose for which income was received.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter to William Smiland regarding disqualification requirements as a South Coast Air Quality Management District Board member given his financial interests in paint companies, a law firm, and prior representation of the Ad Hoc Committee of Small California Paint Manufacturers. The FPPC provides detailed guidance on seven disqualification scenarios, including decisions affecting competitors, substitute products, fees, budgetary matters, and rulemaking issues.
